arch: client round-robin across per-GPU pinned Ollama daemons
note: FALLBACK RUN. gpu_8x_h100_sxm5 had zero capacity in every Lambda region across the full ~30min poll window (all 8x and 4x H100 types were also empty). The persistent FS 'perseus-vault-fs-south' is region-locked to us-south-2, so an out-of-region 4xH100 could not be used. Per the benchmark's fallback rule, ran on the largest multi-GPU available in-region = 8x Tesla V100. The prior 8xH100 headline in fleet_8gpu_throughput.json (peak 650.8 eps @ conc64, 22.76x) is RETAINED UNREFRESHED because H100 could not be obtained; this file is a separate, honestly-labeled V100 data point, not an H100 refresh.
scaling_note: scaling_vs_serial = peak_eps / concurrency-1 baseline (432.2 / 6.7). The ratio is large only because the V100's ~150ms single-request embed latency makes the 1-thread baseline low (6.7 eps); it is NOT a claim that V100 scales better than H100. The comparable H100 figure (22.76x) had a faster 28.6 eps 1-thread baseline. The honest scale-out signal is the near-linear rise in aggregate_eps through concurrency 48-64 as the 8 pinned daemons saturate.

recall_hold.note: Recall re-measured to completion on a 1xH100 against the SAME pre-embedded 100k corpus (the 8xV100 run deadlined mid-recall because V100 query-embed latency ~235ms is too slow for 2000 queries x 9 passes). Recall is hardware-independent (depends on the corpus embeddings + query-embed model, and the recall loop is sequential so a fleet does not speed it up). Numbers match the prior single-GPU scale100k.json EXACTLY, confirming fleet embedding throughput costs no accuracy: hybrid recall@5 = 1.0 at 100K.
